stem: A class of 20 students has an average score of 80. What is the sum of all their scores?
solutionStandard: Sum \(= \text{average} \times \text{count} = 80 \times 20 = 1600\).

stem: A class of 10 students has an average score of 70, and a second class of 20 students has an average score of 85. What is the average score of both classes combined?
Group A (5 members) has an average of 60. Group B (15 members) has an average of 90.
quantityA: The combined average of both groups
solutionStandard: Combined average \(= \dfrac{5(60)+15(90)}{20} = \dfrac{300+1350}{20}=\dfrac{1650}{20}=82.5\). Since Group B is 3 times larger, the combined average leans toward 90, giving 82.5, which is greater than 75. Quantity A is greater.

stem: A store sold 30 shirts at $20 each and 20 shirts at $35 each. What was the average price per shirt, in dollars?
A student's average on 4 tests is 85. What score is needed on a 5th test to raise the average to 88?
solutionStandard: Current sum \(=4(85)=340\). Needed sum for 5 tests at 88: \(5(88)=440\). Fifth score: \(440-340=100\).

stem: A company has 8 employees earning an average of $50,000 and 2 managers earning an average of $90,000. What is the average salary of all 10 people, in dollars?
Two classes take the same exam. Class X (10 students) averages 78, and the combined average of both classes (30 students) is 82. What is the average of Class Y (20 students)?
Group A has \(a\) members with average 50. Group B has \(b\) members with average 80, where \(a > b\).
solutionStandard: Since Group A (average 50) has more members than Group B (average 80), the combined average is pulled below the simple midpoint of 65, toward 50. Quantity B is greater.

stem: A jar has red and blue marbles. Red marbles have an average weight of 5 grams, blue marbles average 8 grams, and the overall average weight is 6 grams. If there are 30 marbles total, how many are red?
A trip consists of 60 miles driven at 30 mph and 60 miles driven at 60 mph. What is the average speed for the entire trip?
solutionStandard: Time for first leg: \(60/30=2\) hours. Time for second leg: \(60/60=1\) hour. Total distance \(120\), total time \(3\) hours. Average speed \(=120/3=40\) mph (not the simple average of 30 and 60, since equal DISTANCES, not equal times, were driven at each speed).

stem: A class of {{n}} students has an average score of {{a}}. What is the sum of all their scores?
solutionStandard: Sum \(=\) average \(\times\) count \(= {{a}} \times {{n}} = {{n*a}}\).

stem: Group 1 has {{n1}} items, averaging {{avg-k*n2}} each. Group 2 has {{n2}} items, averaging {{avg+k*n1}} each. What is the average value per item for all items combined?
solutionStandard: Combined average \(= \dfrac{n_1(\text{avg}-kn_2) + n_2(\text{avg}+kn_1)}{n_1+n_2} = \dfrac{(n_1+n_2)\cdot\text{avg}}{n_1+n_2} = {{avg}}\) — the \(k\) terms cancel exactly.

stem: Group A has {{a}} members with an average of {{m1}}. Group B has {{3*a}} members with an average of {{m1+d}}.
quantityA: The combined average of both groups
solutionStandard: Combined average \(= \dfrac{a(m_1) + 3a(m_1+d)}{4a} = \dfrac{4m_1+3d}{4} = m_1 + \dfrac{3d}{4}\). Quantity B is the simple midpoint \(m_1+\dfrac{d}{2}\). Since \(d>0\), \(\dfrac{3d}{4} > \dfrac{d}{2}\) always — Group B (3 times larger) pulls the combined average above the midpoint. Quantity A is greater.

stem: A store sold 40 shirts at $15 each and 10 shirts at $35 each. What was the average price per shirt, in dollars?
A student's average on {{n}} tests is {{a}}. What score is needed on the next test to raise the average to {{a+d}}?
solutionStandard: Current sum \(=n \cdot a\). Needed sum for \(n+1\) tests at average \(a+d\): \((n+1)(a+d)\). Needed score \(=(n+1)(a+d) - na = a+(n+1)d = {{a+(n+1)*d}}\).

stem: A company has {{n1}} employees earning an average salary of {{avg-k*n2}} dollars and {{n2}} managers earning an average salary of {{avg+k*n1}} dollars. What is the average salary of all {{n1+n2}} people, in dollars?
solutionStandard: Total \(= n_1(\text{avg}-kn_2) + n_2(\text{avg}+kn_1) = (n_1+n_2)\cdot\text{avg}\) — the \(k\) terms cancel. Average \(={{avg}}\).

stem: Group A has \(a\) members with average 50. Group B has \(b\) members with average 80, where \(a > b\).
solutionStandard: Since Group A (average 50) has more members than Group B (average 80), the combined average is pulled below the simple midpoint of 65, toward 50. Quantity B is greater.

stem: A jar has red and green marbles. Red marbles have an average weight of 4 grams, green marbles average 9 grams, and the overall average weight is 7 grams. If there are 40 marbles total, how many are green?
A trip consists of 2 hours driven at 30 mph, followed by 2 hours driven at 60 mph (equal TIME, not equal distance, at each speed). What is the average speed for the entire trip?
solutionStandard: Distance \(=2(30)+2(60)=60+120=180\) miles over \(4\) hours. Average speed \(=180/4=45\) mph — this matches the simple average of 30 and 60 because equal TIMES (not equal distances) were spent at each speed.

stem: Class A has average score \(p\) and Class B has average score \(q\), where \(p \neq q\). Class A has more students than Class B.
quantityA: The combined average of both classes
solutionStandard: The combined average always leans toward whichever class's average is weighted more (Class A, the larger class). If \(p>q\), the combined average exceeds the midpoint; if \(p<q\), it falls below the midpoint. Since the relationship between \(p\) and \(q\) isn't given, the relationship cannot be determined.
